faiths.d: frodbrokna was the confirmation of many Zaoranians' worst fears, especially because it happened exactly when the Prophet#! predicted their god's defeat. The people were left listless, wondering how they could possibly continue to function in a world where evil has triumphed over good. Their answer was surprisingly simple: keep functioning as if it hadn't. Many Zaoranians continue to perform the rituals and sing the songs of their ancestors, out of desperate hope that their gestures will one day drive Zaoran to prophesied victory.

The Nolosaanyo believe in the sanctity and preservation of life so that it may ebb and flow with the tides of the world. The only times of year that the faithful consume meat are during the seasonal feasts that guide the Nolosaanyo's spiritual cycle. It is not uncommon for the most devout of followers to take vows of pasificism, forever swearing off violence, even in the face of death.

A southern Kashiryan faith possibly as old as the Dravadari, Yakasaka is the belief in the endless cycle of 'Gendered Ascension'. Every hundred years the Yakasakans maintain that the greatest man and the greatest woman interned in the spiritual afterlife are risen to godhood where they usurp the gender-split pantheon that guides the stars through the sky and the world around the sun, becoming a greater being together and with all those who led before. The Yakasak faith is perhaps most known for being intensely competitive, every believer technically having the ability to be the one to ascend if they manage to prove themselves greater than their peers. To this end, Yakasakan priests attempt to guide followers along paths of inner perfection, often becoming 'tanaradam' to aid their fellows, the southern-Kashiryan word for 'forbidden', accepting failure in the competition so that they are not biased in their guidance. Due to the dual-gendered nature of divinity, Yakasakan rulers reign equally alongside their spouse. Only men may rule men, and women over women, so each takes on the onus of ruling over citizens of their respective gender.

A Ritualist sect born on the Pirate Coast, the Corkkattirkana believe that the climb of can only truly be completed by the greatest of believers. Each follower strives to be greater than their others, as if in competition to decide who will be the one to complete the climb.

The native faith of the Kemsari people, the Kemsar religion is naturalistic and focused on worship of the light-giving moon Iah and the life-giving river Heya. However, the drying up of Heya and the shattering of Iah during the frodbrokna brought with it both religious and social upheaval, and the lands were plunged into chaos. Attempts to resolve this confusing world led to a new, more cynical faith. Iah is now Inpiah, the Dead Moon. The Kemsari no longer pray for Its protection, but that its decaying corpse does not land on them and destroy what little world they have left.

Nobody is sure what spirit drives the minds of its followers. Nobody is sure what they worship. Nobody even knows where the word "Vindeon" comes from. All they know is that it is connected to the Tower, that primordial monstrosity of cyclopean stone that has sat in the desert wastes before any man set foot there. They obsess over it, and any Towers like it around the world. It is not known what they want to do with these Towers, but nobody wants to find out.

Vidvaookeyatra ('Scholar's Journey) is the worship of the hermaphroditic god of knowledge Sarvat; said to be the font of all knowledge and thus the knowledge of creation itself. With origins rooted in Otached's Otachedian Empire, their scholarly caste are to thank for both the technological, societal and historical progress of Kashirya, as well as its numerous calamities

Across aeras, there exist uncolonized regions untouched by man, at least for the present. Be it through magical disaster, sheer inhospitality, or the inexorable advance of the Fog, swathes of the world have been left nearly devoid of human life. Though some scant few oft live in even the most desolate wastelands, any trace of a true society is long since lost in the struggle for individual survival, even if they scrabble at the ruins of a civilization of which they were once a part.

Worshipping the ancient sea god Varoda, Varodist creation myth is one of the oldest in the world. Most known for their ability to tame nautical beasts, the most famous Varodist figure, Prophet Mogurpa, is said to have tamed and rode a kraken for most of his life.

The origins of the Sheharddi faith are shrouded in mystery, and scholars debate whether it is a syncretism of several Sarradonian faiths, a single-god cult that split off from a larger polytheistic faith, or the surviving remnants of a more primordial religion. They believe that the high god Eloi, creator of the heavens, created a race called the Malikiyah, or Messengers. The eldest of these, Eir'Shaytan, created an even lesser race of beings - the humans - to be his servants and to worship him and his six lieutenants as if they were greater than their father. This caused mankind to be expelled from paradise after the kalnaih and only by being honorable and righteous in life can one return to heaven to worship Him for eternity.

The belief that it is only possible to enter the paradise of Anaan through a lifelong journey of mental exploration and purification means that there are very few converts to the Anaanide faith. Their ultimate solution is seen by its members as a drastic, yet necessary action - the kidnapping of those too young to know sin, and raising them among the Anaanides so they may start on the path as soon as possible. Cruel it may be, but is it not a crueler fate to condemn these souls to oblivion?

The Ruidari'un uphold the 'Pillar of Inclusion,' the idea that if one commits a good deed, it doesn't matter if the deed is done in the name of a false god, it still upholds the Pillars of Virtue that keep the Dead God imprisoned. Ruidari societies feature a big influence of the mystical orders and tusuffna organized around them. Very often, these are open to non-believers, and their spiritual masters attempt to work to guide them to Pillars of Virtue rather than attempt superficial conversions.

The Harfi'un, unlike other Wardenite sects believe that the 'Pillars of Virtue' are actual, physical pillars that can be found in the material world, and must be protected. The other Wardenite sects dismiss this interpetation as comically literal, and view any unironic adherents of the Harfi ways as backwards fundamentalists.

The Kathsiti'un, unlike other Wardenite sects believe that there must be complete separation between the church and state, for secular matters corrupt the true spiritual nature of the church. In reality this has led to an extremely independent, politically powerful group of Kathsiti clerics dictating rules to secular lords.

Owing to their great distance from the homeland, the Aversarian priest in eastern Aironoi struggled to maintain any sort of orthodoxy among the faithful. While the Aversarinas Aagiokrata has always been far from consistent among its thousands of local and imperial cults, the mass deviation its easternmost practitioners slid into was cause for such concern that many priests worried they'd slide from the faith altogether. Despite best attempts, however, what would become known as the Aironoian Aagiokrata largely began to reach a level of consistency with its eastern cults instead of the homelands, and soon became a somewhat codified heresy deeply ingrained in Eastern Culture.

Worshippers of Otached, adherents of this faith hold him and his family in a divine light; his descendants said to possess a touch of divinity. The faith also include many philosophical principles from Kashiryan religions, viewing the myriad gods of Kashirya as having been vassals of Chakkar-Otached. They hold a negative opinion of Otached's grandson Baytor, who they believe was corrupted by Yeru-mal to bring ruin to Otached's divine empire.

Daramarjaratanists worship death goddess and wife of Karitkarmaic, Daramarjaratan (originally Damaratjarat) as the ruler of all other gods. Daramarjatanism remains controversial due to their belief in the Pantheon Of Death and the death of all other gods. While seen as a dangerous cult, Daramarjaratan's care more of the affairs of the dead than the living and rarely engage with outsiders. Despite their pacificity, most only remember the infamous results of Daramarjaratan cults discovering dark magics.

The Redland Cults are as mysterious as they are esoteric. Shadowy and insular, the number of times they have interacted with the world as a group can be counted on one hand. Redland Cultists worship dark and unknowable gods, the sound of their names said to be enough to drive foreigners to madness.

A faith as old as the ancient and rocky hillsides of the Tarsanides - or at least, human habitation thereof, the Tulharsin, or "family" is the name the Barzawa give to their pantheon. Though lesser gods come and go, the core five dieties of the Tulharsin have remained the same since time out of memory, their faces carved by ancient hands into the highest peaks of the Tarsanides Range. Chief among them is Ke-Ren, the mother-father god who split open their own stomach to birth the universe, and from whose viscera crawled the second-born - The most important were the four eldest gods, Tarhannu, Mishkush, Annira, and Kesh, as well as the moon itself. Once assembled, these siblings then set about populating aeras by bringing into existence all manner of flora, fauna, and terrible monsters, alongside man.

The Tathtali'a have pretensions to being the mainstream branch of wardenism, and historically this assumption hasn't been altogether incorrect as the Tathtali'a hold sway over the most heavily populated core of the Wardenite faith. Though they would claim their traditions date back centuries to the founding of Wardenism under the First Warden himself, the quirks that define the movement owe more to the 9th century IS Mamur#! whose name the movement is often labeled with. The major point of divergence between the Tathtalites and what came before is in the unified and all powerful figure of the Mamur, whose rule is absolute and sacred. It is he who has final right of dissent in all legal matters, and he who leads the armies of the faithful in times of war and settles the soldiers in times of peace. His word is absolute - or at least, such is the theory.

Aversarinas Aagiokratia is the catch-all term for the assorted denominations, offshoots, and heresies of the Aversarinas Aautokrata and the Imperial Cult. Though their tenets may vary wildly, they largely revolve around belief in the primordial 'First Men', a divine race of magic-wielding humans led by a magi named the Purest who once ruled the World City and the First Empire. The Aagiokrata is a religion largely without any cohesive belief system, and is instead dominated by innumerable cults often descended from innumerable faiths long conquered and absorbed by the Empire.

The Sophocists emphasize the memorization and performance of symbolic divine rituals, which they insist are the true Thousand-and-One Steps of . The Tower where the physical Steps lay are merely where the lessons were passed down, and the rituals were meant to be performed anywhere. Through accurate performance of all 1,001 rituals, will a person be able to understand the secrets of the gods and attain enlightenment.

A cult founded in the wake of the Frodbrokna. The Anban is a deliberate and somewhat cynically pragmatic attempt to unify both Wardenism and Ritualism into a singular faith based around the figure of an immaterial godhead lawgiver. Partially a political philosophy, its idealistic proponents offer it as a method to unite Sarradon and usher in a new age of religious peace. For somewhat obvious reasons it has generally struggled to find popular support outside of a handful of warlords looking for any kind of edge over their rivals.

The Road of the One was founded by a prophetess named Hanaa, a woman born under Wardenite rule. Hanaa was born a Ritualist, but fraternized extensively with Wardenites. Attaining an understanding of Wardenite beliefs, and sympathizing with the Wardenite cause, Hanaa was said to have been given a revelation when she ascended the steps of , warning her that the physical world was created by a malevolent demiurge, and that it this demiurge's servants must be combated whenever possible. Only through its annihilation can mortals be freed from the shackles of physical existence and dwell eternally in the Gardens of Life. Hanaa also preached that there was but one god, and that this god was named the One. The One commanded the Ritualists to battle the demiurge long ago, but these prophets were misinterpreted or slain by their followers; therefore, the One gave a revelation unto the first of the Wardenites, and abandoned the Ritualists. But if the Ritualists heed Hanaa, and live according to the Road of the One - a list of laws delivered to Hanaa - the One will favor them again.

The Followers of Zatanai adhere to the teachings of the Ritualist prophet Zatanai, who received his revelations from at just seventeen years of age. Zatanai, though young, was a fierce orator who stated that the Wardenites must be destroyed utterly, and that anyone who rejected the truths of should be slain as an enemy of mankind. Though it was not originally meant to be a permanent sect, Zatanai incorporated elements of the Suleimani'a sect into his own, chiefly the belief in Domah Daniyal and the view that all philosophies can be learned from, though Zatanai rejected the idea that non-Ritualist philosophies have any merit. Zatanai and his followers believe that Ritualism is the one and only way of life for mankind; all other religions are mere perversions of Ritualism, and so must either be assimilated if possible or destroyed if not. Zatanai was one of the youngest Ritualist prophets in the religion's history, and he entered into occultation at nineteen years of age after declaring that he would return when his people needed him most.

At the age of sixteen, every Marcher is supposed to choose a Path (or more controversially, have one forced by a parent or lord) that dictates the role they are to play in both Marcher Society and the world. They are tutored in this path by a Waymaster, one skilled in their role who has dedicated their life to teaching others. Initiates study the ways of the revered Waysaints of the past, who guide the spirits in aiding the faithful down their Paths. For most of the faith's existence, its priesthood has organized beneath the .

Worshipping mainly the god of war, death and justice Kartikarma over other Avastya gods, the Karitkarmaicists believe in the necessity of war, death and peace; a necessary cycle upon which society and the world always turn. While not actively seeking out war and death, they believe it also shouldn't be rejected and instead embraced as part of the universal experience, forming peaceful yet stoic realms

The Sunarwei Aagiokrata is perhaps one of the most peculiar results of the Aversarianization of a native faith to improve stability of Aversaria's vast empire. While normally Aversarianization of a faith involves modifying the faith to exalt the Aversarians and magi in some form in order to quell dissent, the Sunarwei Aagiokrata instead attempts to encourage more militaristic outlooks and push back against its traditional pacifistic beliefs while making almost no mention of Aversarians or magi.

A prominently insular faith centered around pacifism and kindness, the Sunarweiaa believe in the whole world being made of the creator god Kumhant. Turning himself into trillions of shards of glass after passing too close to the sun, these shards became all things; living and non-living. As such, the Sunarweiaa have a very pacifistic philosophy; if all things are remnants of the same great god, then all are equal, and deserving of respect.

Iram, the City of the Pillars. She will rise from the sands like dawn, but only for those whose minds are open and hearts are worthy. She will be black as pitch, and engraved with gold, full of jewels that glitter like a thousand stars. The Seven-Headed Serpent will watch you longingly within the city, where you will be safe. Do not seek Iram, faithful one, until Iram has sought for you.

Formed after the events of Chan Skorpokaipis and the Dharya Varha, Askevaryaa shares almost the same gods and tenets of Vidvaookeyatra. Where it diverges, however, is the inclusion of so called Evil Lore.

Although Axiaothea#! and her most powerful supporters were all killed by the despicable Aeschraes#! when he ushered in the frodbrokna, the late Empress still has her supporters within the former borders of the Empire. They revere her legacy nearing on deification, painting her struggle with Aeschraes as a battle between good and evil for the fate of the world. And unfortunately, evil got the upper hand this time.

A rationalistic and experiential philosophy founded by the Adar the Nib, a notoriously irascible scholar and philosopher of the late 8th century blooming of the Mamural courts. Initially working within the confines of an intellectually liberated form of Traditionalist Wardenism, Adar`s theories evolved to the extent that they effectively rendered him an apostate. His followers to this day are saddled with the label "Hulaw", meaning "truant" after the wording of a judgement by the High Court of the Center shortly following his death. The insult from the courts was ultimately impotent and without Mamural backing, and his heirs quickly began to self-identify with the label out of a shared love of ironic humor.

In the faith of the Shub-Yuaguag, conception and motherhood are seen as sacred acts that imitate the nature of the Mother of Milk. However, that means that women are expected to always serve as mothers and homemakers, leaving them confined to domesticity while men manage affairs outside of the home. The end result is a faith with a deep gender imbalance, where fathers have the right to even order the deaths of their wives and children for defying sacred commands.

Andaronshiaks believe gods are an unknowable force, manifesting in humanity as magic. Through perfection of virtues, one shall gain control over magic through their gods, whether in current life or the next. Acknowledging the path to magic as a personal journey, Andaronshiaks have a bitter hatred for religious formalism, considering it antithetical to their holy goals.

Cuthanagi believe the Kasiryat to be a mortal mirror to a river present in the realm of the divine, with the gods unknowable and without forms or names. Instead, the state of the river is observed as a way of divine prophesy; claiming joyous mood of the gods when the Kasiryat floods, leaving behind great fertility, and coming apocalypse when stricken with drought. Most notably reflected by great celebrations during flood times, with thousands of paper candles being sent drifting down the river.
Devoted to ;medium Saint Sansia#!, Sansianites emphasize self-sacrifice and denial of worldly pleasures in the name of spiritual salvation. Their symbol is a rose, stained by their patroness' blood.

Believing in ninety books of 'positive' enlightenment and one of 'neutral' enlightenment that leads to ascension after reading them, the faith's origins can be traced to a guru named pethyka. After Dharya Varha, the ninety-one books were deemed to contain the only true knowledge in this world, with everything else said to be a creation of man.
Followers of ;medium Saint Philantros#!, Philantrosianites collect alms from the wealthy and distribute them to those in need. Their symbol is a gilded hand, stretched outward in charity.

The Hubarists treat magi, who received their gifts from the Sun, as keepers of its will and enforcers of the law. This system of Magi-judges was effective since time immemorial, but its fortunes have reversed recently. Centuries of religious conflict with the Wardenites, Ritualists, and finally the Agionists have greatly diminished their power, and the Hubarists are now a shadow of what they once were.

Iek Otached ('Great Otached') is an Öltenic faith centered around the Öltenic warlord Otached from which most modern Öltenic culture and religion (as well as their very entrance to relevance to The Continent) have descended from. While contemporarious to Otached's life, there is little record of Otached actually patronizing his cult or paying it any mind at all, instead gaining traction upon his death and declaring him a God; a physical reflection of himself.

The isles of the southeast have been under the rule of aversaria since before the Chaos Of The Seventh Century and though considered to be irrelevant by many, the Aautokratir Varthanius endeavoured greatly to consolidate imperial control over the area in order to safeguard the passing between the fledgling province of Aironoi and Opakhasia. Part of his initiatives were the subduing of the Zaragett, masters of water magics who had lived in the area from time immemorial and had fiercely resisted the initial Aversarian conquest. The result was the Zaragett Aagiokrata, a faith that elevates the magi priesthood to a position of supreme power over both the temporal and spiritual worlds. Furthermore, designated god-kings were to serve as high priests, placing them above the rest of the priesthood and rule over the faithful as incarnates of the Seamother and Moonfather. Typically the reigning Aautokratir of Aversaria is held to be the god-king of the Zaragett, though in recent times a great many warlords have taken to making use of the title in the pursuit of ever greater power.

The Slaves of the Great Worm are worshipers of the The Worldeater and what they believe to be its innumerable spawn deep beneath aeras. Slaves of the Great Worm worship the Worldeater as the Great Worm, or al-Duhaad Khubir, and its progeny as the Great Worms, or al-Diyhaadan al-Eazaam. It is only through providing the Great Worms sacrifices and worship that they may be kept slumbering beneath the world, and the Slaves take it as their duty to prevent another Great Worm from awakening again.
Calystianites revere ;medium Saint Calysto#!, who led numerous liberation wars against the Aversarians. Their symbol is two crossed keys, representing freedom from both temporal and spiritual bonds.

Born of a belief that horses are too sacred to waste in war, the strict rites of the Mshvirat restrict the use of the holy animals to races and long-distance travel. Their warrior rites instead follow a slower pace on foot, appearing more a dance than a raid in how carefully each step is executed. In peace, however, few can match the joyous atmosphere of their equestrian games.
Dedicated to ;medium Saint Eos#!, the Forlorn Guardian, Eosians seek to emulate his courage both on and off the battlefield. Their symbol is a sword, blazing with righteous fury

The Mahrator religion is mostly animistic, worshiping a large host of horse-spirits hailed as being almost equivalent to the gods. Kaegan, God of Horses and King of Heaven, is said to have taught the first men how to ride horses with the aid of Horsemother, who once rode across the sky until the horse-hating goddess Ur-mai threw her down to the earth Horses are revered as being quasidivine, and are given lavish funerals upon death. To harm a horse is a grievous sin punishable by death. The Mahratorans also believe that a man's worth is proven only through combat, and so they conduct ritual raids against their enemies. Both horses and men are given sky burials so that Ur-mai cannot steal their souls.

The Aversarianzed version of the thousands of Amaghean faiths, the Amaghean Aagiokrata greatly emphasizes the role of the magi (especially those of Aversarian blood) in the Amaghean faith and their ability to ascend to godhood alongside great warriors of old. Even further, the Amaghean Aagiokrata holds that Magi can transcend to an even higher plane of existence than the other gods and be ever closer to Arnaz the "Great Ancestor" and rule eternally from heavenly paradise. Warriors are still more often exalted, though the faith emphasizes that this is through showing "aspects of Magi" and that they will rarely, if ever, be able to rise quite as high as those with the gift. This faith came about when Aversaria pacified the Amaghean coasts; however, it did not take major traction until the reign of Dedaphal Mariamne 'the Builder,' who would patronize the Amaghean Aagiokrata and turn it into its modern incarnation.

The Suleimanii follow the teachings of the magi-philosopher Suleiman the Wise, who posited that the on the surface of the world is an emanation of the True Sophocos maintained deep beneath the earth in the caverns of Domah Daniyal. The Suleimanii believe that long ago, men were blessed with great wisdom and stature, and that their philosophies allowed them to manipulate the world. Over time they began to degenerate in size and wisdom, and their philosophy likewise degenerated into what is now called magic, becoming dangerous and enticing men to evil. A few called the Banu Daniyal fled underground to the True Sophocos, while their kinsmen transformed into modern man. Suleimanii believe philosophy is divine and philosophers are the incarnations of divinity on aeras.

The Öltenis believe that if they leave their faces exposed for too long, jealous Yeru-mai will identify them as Kalgan's children and steal their souls away. Thus, they conceal themselves with facemasks, which they always wear when outside or in the company of others. These masks are only removed during intimate or ritually important moments, as a sign of pure loyalty and vulnerability.

Katharites adhere to the teachings of ;medium Saint Katharitos#!, a travelling preacher who told his followers to be pure in flesh and in thought. Their symbol is their founder's staff, to remind them of his journeys.

The Akhawwat al-Iiraahadat al-Mutajaaluyah - the Fraternity of the Will Manifest - is an esoteric Ritualist sect that believes that reality is a collective hallucination, and that true reality is actually a spiritual realm. Humans are not beings of fallible flesh, but are rather immortal and immaterial spiritual entities that are eternal but of a finite creation. They have trapped themselves in this false reality through the deceits of al-Khadidruj, but may learn the truth through Sophocos - which itself does not exist - and regain control of their spiritual bodies and break the delusion of existence.

The Sabiat al-Malaikiya was founded by the prophetess Sarah bint Shakira, who reputedly ascended the steps of and was greeted by a host of angels who were horrible to behold and whose voices caused men to enter into convulsions. Sarah was accompanied by a band of other Ritualists who reported seeing the angels as well. Speaking to their prophetess, the angels commended the Ritualists for their devotion to , and gave Sarah a revelation: the gods and goddesses of this world were but masks worn by the angels of , and each religion was but another path to enlightenment. She was therefore charged with delivering peace to men, to cease the endless wars of Sarradon and bring an understanding between Ritualist and Wardenite, and then to spread this message of peace to the world entire. She and her new converts called themselves the Tribe of the Angels, and set about spreading this message of coexistence.

Khourtaulians beleive that the Feathered One will not ferry their souls to Paradise directly. Instead, they believe that the flesh of the dead must be eaten first so that the soul can drift into Paradise and the embrace of Chitdada. They have traditionally been a small and aggresive sect, and often treat their more mainstream kin as apostates that have chosen to forget the truth.

Dikaynosites are named for ;medium Saint Dikaynos#!, a judge so devoted to the law that he committed the entire legal code to memory after being blinded so he could continue his duties. Their symbol is a closed eye, demonstrating that physical sight is unnecessary to see truth.

The Quwazawi treat the titanic stone statues they worship with utmost reverence: they wash them, repaint the intricate designs on them, and even weave great cloaks for them that take entire villages a year to produce. The main driving force for these actions, dismissed by outsiders as foolish wastes of time and resources, is the confidence that they will be rewarded for their piety and duty when their gods reawaken and reassert their rule over mankind.

A paramount heresy among most of their Wardenite coreligionists, Badari'a is the result of an attempted syncretism with Dikansynite religious laws which was promulgated by a faction within the Mamur Radi's court. Generally disliked and seen as radicals despite their repeated attempts to demonstrate otherwise, the Badarites are barely even tolerated by their Agionist 'friends', who see them as more of a bad joke than as serious allies.

Alau Koevars believe that the world of the gods was shut off from them, and that the passage will only open once again when the world is ready to be cleansed of its sins in fire. In preparation for the re-opening of the Fire Gate, pious adherents burn themselves ritualistically in preparation for the return of Pleimao to aeras. Alau Koevars enjoy the regular disgust of outsiders for their faith, and believe that their suffering through life is evidence of the divine fire within themselves.

Believing themselves to be a mere flock of sheep guided by a shepherd, Xax'baxa'kanaxi hold no power over their spiritual . Due to their extremely esoteric nature, they have a very primitive relation with the world around them

Meaning "regency", the A'Arayi were born when Tathtali#! joined the Eastern and Western Mamuramats, bringing most of the East's beleaguered courts with him. A few of these courts, however, refused to follow. Clustering around the High Court Judge turned Warlord Afnaan, they refuted Tathtali's claim to right by marriage. In absence of any legitimate candidate to serve in Tathtali's stead, the High Court of the East - an institution formed at the behest of Afnaan - would serve as regent until such a time as a rightful heir to the East could be found. Given the complete apparent extinction the Zaahirah family's matrilineal line - apart from Sanad's children via Tathtali who were illegitimate by way of their father's usurpation - this last goal has proven somewhat difficult. The High Court of the East have propped up a handful of hopeful pretenders to the throne throughout the centuries, and though they have not yet succeeded, the stubborn Arayi Judges believe it's only a matter of time until the Tathtalite Heresy is overthrown.

Thysians venerate ;medium Saint Thysia#!, a renowned healer who was executed for selflessly treating both master and slave. Their symbol is a chalice filled with blood, a symbol of both the practice of bloodletting and Thysia's sacrifice.

Hukadirites believe that the world came into being from the swirling maelstrom of Fawhadaa, uncreated by any intelligent being. There all manner of beasts emerged, and prime among them was mankind. But men were without gods, and so they were without morality; they set about to create their own gods, and their own morality, but were unsuccessful. Thither came Qubaal, the First Vampire, who successfully made himself a god. Qubaal then instructed others on how to achieve divinity, and the first vampires ruled over a magnificent empire called , from which Qubaal ruled at his great spire. Eirbaadit al-Hukadir operates under a euhemeristic framework, assuming that all gods have a mortal origin; they are either non-existent or were otherwise mortals who became deified. While the former is less valid than the latter, what ultimately matters is whether someone pays the Blood Tithe or not.

The mainstream Mogowai, codified in the Karururi Chorus, take their task of finding the body of the Most High incredibly seriously. While a good many just pray that He leads His followers to His final resting place, others are compelled to go out into the world and search themselves. They retread old paths thought to be dead ends, or search in completely undiscovered locales, and pursue any lead about a sacred burial site in hopes of fulfilling their one goal. It is believed that when found, He will rise once more, and transform the world into a paradise.

While other denominations believe in centralized efforts to seek out His body, the Marefai decry central religious authority entirely, believing that mere mortals are unworthy to dictate the orders of the Most High. They encourage everyone to live their lives in accordance with their own interpretations of the Hymns, and see those who would attempt to lead the faith as usurpers. Despite their variety of beliefs, the quest for His tomb remains paramount, along with most other core tenets.

The Waharai hold that the Most High's death was merely His ascension, and that He waits in the heavens for those who accept this truth. Their attitude towards piety is far more lax than their fellows, to the Waharai, your actions on aeras matter not, as merely the belief in the Most High will earn you a reward. As a result, the rules they follow are less formal and structured, with far more leeway given to individuals that would have been punished by other faiths, so long as they maintain their beliefs.

The Ḥārthahi sect was created in response to perceived Wardenite weakness, and a belief that the Wardenite sects are too tolerant of outsiders. The Ḥārthahi'un argue that the Dead God is imprisoned within sarradon, and that all evil done upon its land is a direct attempt to unleash them back upon the world. Thus, Sarradon must be free, and its people united against those the Ḥārthahii view as foreign invaders - namely the Agionists, Aversarians, and marchers. While militant, the faith is fairly pluralistic, more concerned with removing the realms of hostile foreigners than their populations.

Izáerto Bošár, meaning 'Thousand Masters' is a faith centered around the belief that eons ago a thousand shadow-skinned god-kings were sent down from the uncaring and eldritch chief god named the 'Great Void'. Though their complicated creation myth is often the source of many foreign insults, the holy men and women take little heed. The Great Feathered One will take their souls to paradise upon their death, so no amount of mortal mockery can break the faithful's spirits.

Daminkarites believe that Alau Koev itself is God, a singular and mighty force that transported them into the mortal world from the dark prison known as Ada Tvi. They are known for their lavish festivals of fire, where the faithful feast upon charred meat and dance across long rows of flaming coals. To them, the mortal world is a blessing to be enjoyed, and the re-opening of the Fire Gate means fear, because it signals the return of the Cold Master Khel Tvi to claim their souls.

The Kamayed believe that the Thousand-and-One Steps of are indeed a literal place, but not at the great Tower that is usually described as their location in Sophocist folklore. Instead, they claim that the steps are a location of descent into a great crater "beyond the north stars." This crater, they insist, is the doorway to the afterlife, and those who are worthy enough to reach the bottom may enter into the realm of the gods.

Long ago, Lion, the mightiest of beasts, was alone in the world. With a mighty roar humanity emerged from the soil, and Lion was alone no longer. Then came Hound, guardian of the dead and warriors. Hound was enraged at Lion, for Lion had no care for the lives of the humans he had created, and no loyalty to his creation. Before there could be a great war between the two, Lynx tricked Lion into inviting Hound into his home. As the entire aeras was Lion's home, he was bound by the rules of hospitality, and thus Lion and Hound were bound together as brothers.

The Tsokarim worship Tsokan, the God of Knowledge, as their chief deity. To them, there is nothing greater than the pursuit of the forbidden knowledge of the world, and donate much of their time travelling across Aironoi in search of answers to the most pressing questions. Whilst their charred skin may deter outsiders from conversing with the Tsokarim, they are often regarded as the most open-minded of all Alau Koevars.

All details about the ;medium Saint#! this cult worships, even their name, have been lost to time, so adherents wear masks and discourage personal praise in emulation of their patron's lack of identity. Their symbol is the eye of their saint, staring vacantly, unable to focus until their name is uttered once again.

A polytheistic faith with an old history, gods are ephemeral creators who can live, die, be reborn, and experience effects of entropy and negentropy like humans. Paerasjatan (often forgotten in modern times) is the god of creation magic who is said to have been the one to make the first iteration of the world, with many old myths about Them.

Founded by disgruntled peasants, the Mafida believe that all humans have the right to interpret and translate the Sacred Texts, from the lowest of peasants to the highest of kings. Due to the large variety of interpretations of the Sacred Texts tolerated by the Mafida, they have been labelled as heretical by all other Manats, even if the majority of Mafida have similar beliefs to the Manat mainstream.

Much of modern Manat religious culture is built around protection and interpretation of the Sacred Texts of the gods, which is overseen by a large caste of Translator Priests. Although there are original translations passed down by oral tradition, many Manat translators attempt to decipher the original language of the gods themselves, so that they might better be able to form a society as it was in those holiest of early days.

Meaning 'Air Worship', the faith is an umbrella term for Öltenic faiths claiming their gods are reflected on aeras through the actions of air and wind, resulting from the gods interacting with the world. They eschew normal attempts at divination in return for obsessive observation of the movements of the wind, resulting in defeat even at the brink of victory due to changing wind patterns.

The Gallicader were once the dominant faith of chevalie, having been displaced only relatively recently by the Pathtreaders. They believe that man has no free will of his own, and that all of life from birth to death is dictated by the movements of the stars. By observing the celestial orbits, one may begin to uncover the secrets of the future and the lost knowledge of the past.

The Bekashai hold that those who slew the Most High must be brought to justice, believing that only through attaining revenge can His body be found. They have refocused their efforts to search the world, ignoring the quest for His body itself and instead seeking out His killers, hoping to one day avenge their ancient sin. A variety of theories as to His death have been proposed, but most tend to simply blame one's current enemies for the long-ago tragedy

The Nitsafai believe that the mainstream Mogowai have lost their way, due largely to the meddling of secular leaders in religious affairs. They push for a literal interpretation of the Hymns of Heaven, and pursue a refocusing of the faith before the search can resume, fearing that corruption will prevent His body from ever being discovered. This dedication causes them to uphold their orthodoxy above all else, and any deviation from their truth is harshly punished.

The strange and somewhat mysterious beliefs of the tribes of the western crater, Ahridar is the worship of the ancient god-king Ohrasik, who freed humanity from slavery at the hands ancient devils who once filled the world with shadow. Ohrasik slew the devils and enslaved many of them to follow his will, but the scars the devils left on the world never went away, becoming shadow and night and resisting Ohrasik who would take the form of the sun in the sky. Ahridarists maintain that Ohrasik is an extremely active god despite the silence he gives his faithful, Ohrasik simply so focused on his daily war against shadow that he is unable to answer the majority of the prayers of the faithful. Many Ahridarist warlords have claimed to be malaak, or Ohrasik incarnate, Ahridarists claiming that Ohrasik will sometimes assist his faithful by taking human form and working to slay the servants of darkness. Most rulers who claim this title proclaim "It is I who will slay the moon" upon their proclamation, believing aervalr to be the evil god who directs the devils against Ohrasik. Aversarian scholars have noted that the Ahridarist oral tradition seems to reference multiple events that did not actually occur anywhere near the crater, but in ancient aversaria. Despite this, no further link has anyone been found, and the faith has remained an exceptionally interesting topic in Aversarian scholarly circles, though is far too remote and removed from Aversarian affairs for serious notice to be spared.

The Gestanwoder once ruled over the Konijkmener swamps, but then they were pushed out by the Pathtreader invaders. They survive only within the deepest and hardest to reach marshlands, clinging to their ancestral faith and believing this to simply be another punishment from their cruel gods. But some have turned to dark and untold creatures in places where even the fearless dare not tread, seeking brief moments of untold power in their short lives...

Founded on the belief that the spirit of the Sun-Moon-King resides only in the souls of the faithful, Ertomaxism is named in veneration of the great ertomax, who is revered as the most faithful of all. Believed to be the only human to pass in life, his death at the hands of a nameless assassin did nothing quell the fervor of his zealous supporters, who now merely await for his inevitable return.

The Gravewardens are those that worship a modernized version of the Graveright, created in an attempt to ensure the Graveright's survival with the modern world. Instead of necropolis complexes where the dead are stored, and buildings made of their bones, the dead are watched over for a week as priests perform rituals of purification and rest. Gravewardens claim these rites will still allow the soul to protect an area as long as they continue their memory and build markers commemorating them in death. These grave sites are tightly guarded by temple guardians, each individual body looked over by a cadre of guards who ensure that they are not disturbed. While far more acceptable to modern eyes, it has struggled to find converts among the Marches or convince the old believers of their vision, and has struggled to take hold.

Only the most purified are permitted to see the greatest statues with their own eyes. It is the priests who go into trances in the temples' cells, listening for commands from the gods beneath the gold and relaying it into intelligible orders for secular 'rulers'. Above all has been one command, repeated by the gods throughout the centuries: go forth and conquer, so that all the world may join their supplication.

'Hero Cult' is a catch-all term for thousands of minor faiths tied together by a common belief system which places great mortals as the forefront object of worship, originating in the Western Isles. Though its legends may occasionally include mentions of disinterested deities and world-shaking leviathans, most Hero Cult pantheons are composed almost entirely of men and women of impressive deeds who are perceived to have some connection to local history - though this connection can often be quite tenuous, debatable or outright invented from whole cloth. Despite some propensity to exalt great warriors more often than heroes of other backgrounds, tales of the exploits of great adventurers, traders, scholars, rogues and magi are no less treasured than their more martial counterparts.

The Militant Cliques are an Aversarian cult originating from within its Legions based primarily around the worship of the Agionist Saint Sansia (as the embodiment of strategic genius) and of Saint Eos (as the embodiment of martial perfection). The Saints had many sympathizers (and outright followers) within the Legions, and in the recovery from the Agionist Rebellions there was no political will to attempt to dislodge them from within the otherwise loyal Legions. As such, worship of the Saints has always been seen as somewhat controversial yet extremely common, and most soldiers carry icons of Sansia#! or Eos#! as good luck charms even if they do not consider themselves faithful. Some Legionnaires have gone so far as to venerate them both solely as two halves of the Purest, and almost all veteran Legionnaires in Aversaria have historically considered themselves in some way associated to these beliefs.

Following the frodbrokna and the fracturing of aversaria, not all of the Imperial Priesthood were content to lose power. In fact, some sought to consolidate it. They rallied behind one of their own and proclaimed him the Ypsilonis Omitilos, demanding that every other ecclesiastical and secular ruler show deference. They want a strong head around whom the faithful can rally... and weak kings who are incapable of getting in their way.

Once the dominant faith of Krehejad, E'hat'm is a belief in a duotarian goddess of Ehatm Sister Mother. For centuries islanders centered around the Warrior Women Ehatm until their defeat during War Of All Islands.

Drakánorix Aagiokrata (literally 'Dragon Faith' in Aversarian languages) is an ancient Aversarian belief system predating most known memory and any form of Aversarian writing systems, held by believers as the original faith of the Aversarian peoples. What is known is that the oldest found piece of Aversarian writing, the Drakánoramonicar, was created by the faith's ancient leaders to codify their faith. Drakánorix Aagiokrata worships the dragons of ancient Aversaria, deifying them as beings of pure magic and divinity, sent to the world by a forgotten being to rule man. While extremely disconnected and spread across much of the world (cults being found as far as sarradon), the faith nonetheless dominated much of what is now the Imperial Isle, Etepezea, and the Shattered Sea. It would remain a pillar of Aversarian society even after the formation of the Aversarinas Aagiokratia, only falling to the wayside after the War Of The Thousand Dragons. Ironically, they would serve the Imperial faction in the Civil War, their long-running hatred of Aersodiaxianism throwing them alongside the Aagiokrata. After their victory, Lethigianex would slaughter all remaining dragons, and those remaining cultists with them. The final high priest of the Dragon Cults would attempt a final uprising under Aautokratia Maklea#!, in the midst of her purges, and would be flayed and nailed to the wall of her palace with the desecrated horns of dragons.

Astrikayans oppose most religious norms of conventional faiths and preach mundanity in all things, divine or mortal. From the seas to skies to even dragons and magic. The faithful spend their time endlessly debating philosophical concepts and works of philosophers like saru that sometimes bring great hostility, and they can be found in most Kashiryan courts.

Arthenaxianists worship arthenax, the last known Dragon in aversaria. Three centuries after the purge and believed extinction of dragons, Arthenax and her brood entered Aversaria and pillaged the realm for nearly a decade before being slain by Aautokratia Aeliadias. Arthenax in her pride demanded worship, and while her cult was never popular it has nonetheless proven resilient.

First appearing after the Chaos Of The Seventh Century with the rise of the mayikrata, Practitioner Aagiokrata is a cult which believe that the Aversarinas Aagiokratia is flawed for allowing the profanity of naming non-magi as divine. Adherents often either lock themselves in their towers devoting their time to research, in pursuit of learning more about the intricacies of magic and perfecting it, or work on turning aversaria into the ideal magocracy. Though derided as ambitious pretenders with limited legitimate skills by some, declaring oneself for the cult is seen as a showing of strength among the powerful (and not so powerful) Magi nobles.

The ancient Cult of the Dragon is a faith native to the more southwestern shatteredisles, those closest to the continent of sarradon. A mixture of beliefs and doctrines from both Aersodiaxianism and the Drakánorix Aagiokrata, the Cult of the Dragon focuses solely on the worship of those great beasts famed across aeras for their might and majesty. They exhort the divine attributes of dragonkind, and insist that the creator god, Karalthor created those known as the Winged Lords in their own image to rule over mankind as benevolent monarchs and vicious scourges of injustice and impurity. The greatest of the Winged Lords, Paratharian stands above all others as the firstborn child of Karalthor and overlord of not only all Dragons but of Aeras itself. A litany of other Dragons make up the rest of the Cult's pantheon, though a great number of these 'Dragons' are in truth simply exceptionally potent magi deified after death.

After the frodbrokna, the inhabitants of several coastal islands became obsessed with the sea. They have adapted their rituals to be performed facing the shore, and their priests try and divine the future by observing the patterns of clouds and waves during storms. They insist that they are still just as faithful as any other Aagiokratan, but suddenly clam up when asked by an outsider to explain the details of their faith.

Hated by all, feared by many, scourges of the Shattered Coast and all its Isles, the Seaborne Exalted are an abhorrent and spiteful cult who value the act of raiding and pillaging above all else. Believing that all of mankind arose from seasalt, to the Seaborne a life without murder, plunder, and rape is one devoid of any meaning. Life is short, and when one dies there is no lofty afterlife or place of heavenly residence but only the returning of one's body to the seasalt it was before. Thus, one should seize any opportunities that might arise, commit any crimes one might wish to commit and take all that one can, for nothing lies beyond the veil of death besides an eternity of being carried from shore to shore by the perfidious sea that once troubled them so much in life. To kill is to exert one's will on this world of the damned and change the tides of fate themselves. To rule is to be strong, and the weak should stand aside or die when confronted by their betters. No law, no god, and no king constrains the Seaborne, who come where they will and go when they may, and woe betide any who seek to chain them.

An exotic branch of wardenism cut off from the Wardenite heartlands, the Stranûni beliefs have steadily diverged into something truly strange. The Stranûnites hold that words and the structure of those words hold great power, and that the pillars that keep the Dead God trapped are in fact five different holy forms of poetry and music. They hold that these forms must be constantly practiced, with poet-judges required to know at least one of the styles. However, the Stranûnites view it as of utmost importance that these sacred forms are not misused and only practiced under the proper circumstances. This leads to a paradoxical society, where music and poetry is simultaneously omnipresent and tightly regulated.

The Xwestrozên arose with Naza the Great after she received a revelation on the true nature of . The Xwestrozên claim that Sophocos is not, in fact, a real place, but rather a song with one thousand and one notes, each note rising upward in pitch. Supposedly, mastery of this song will grant one enlightenment and great magical skill, with the Xwestrozên believing that magi draw their power from knowledge of some notes of the song. However, all music is considered holy to the Xwestrozên, not just the Song of Sophocos, and musicians are widely regarded as pious individuals.

The Aegispact was the legendary vow made by the twelve refugee kings and queens of the mainland and the thirty-and-one tribal chieftains of Lonely Light that ended the 'Bloody Feuds' - the period of mass bloodshed as refugees poured into the west to flee the Sorrowing Of Oejeynica and attempted to establish dominion over the local tribes and the other fleeing mainlanders. Formed with the defeat of the hated Priccany and Brynd the Sly's retrieval of the Aegis from the far north, the Pact swore all to agree to a single ruler, chosen by each tribe and each king, dedicated not to personal gain but the protection of the Aegis and the defense of Lonely Light. While the Kingdom has risen and fallen many times since, the many disparate religions have long faded into one another, united not by their faith in gods but in their faith in the power of the might Aegis.

Shy'atan is the western Amaghean belief that time is ultimately nothing more than a reflection of an unending cosmos and ultimately is nothing besides yet another facet of the world that humans can shape to their will, just as they can dig a ditch or planet a tree. Shy'atani priests claim to be endless reincarnations of other priests who discovered a way to step out of the cycle of time and reenter it in a new body. Priests before their 'death' will seemingly randomly choose a time along with village, region, or name, and declare that to be the date and location of their reincarnation, a babe then being plucked from there (sometimes forcefully) to be raised among the priests to return to life. While this is normally seen as a great honor, it is still considered heresy to resist the coming priests. Secular rulers have often chafed against the excesses of the priesthood, technically beholden to any demands made by the priests who often miraculously choose their own children as their reincarnations and thus maintain vast holdings in their name.

Saratarat is a pacifistic oceanic religion based around the worship of the saratans of the Kezymin crater. Sarataratians believe that the saratans were the first guardians of the world as well as the first beings to exist, and that their movements formed the seas and aeras which swirled around them to form reality and the universe itself. After great calamity, the faithful swore themselves to pacificism, and now live humble lives as fishermen and merchants.

The Sjalvolki, or "strong folk" in their tongue, consider themselves to be those who still follow the teachings of velos the Great Hunter. They praise the hunt and consider it to be a key tenet of their faith, though this is also coupled with communing with nature and responsibility in their actions. The gods showed them favor by appointing them as stewards of the world, yes, but they shall likely not receive such generosity again if they squander it. Thus, they feel the need to sometimes take up arms against the truly evil and egregious, so as to keep the rest of existence safe.

Calystoanistism is a cult centered around the worship of the Agionist saint, Calysto, known to many as the greatest hero to ever spring forth from the shatteredisles. Originating from the few remnants of the legendary pirate's crew that managed to elude Aversarians and Order Militant both, the Calystoans held fast to the beliefs of their freedom-loving patron, treasuring personal autonomy and liberty above all else. For this they are staunchly opposed to the heretical Saintly Order of Calysto's Key, whom they view as mere slavers and heretics of the most vile breed. Unlike most cults of the isles centered around exceptional heroic figures, Calystoanistism holds Calysto to be a deity who descended from the celestial realm to unite the lost peoples of aeras into one. Though he has now returned to his abode in the cosmos, it is believed that in this action he left the task of completing his holy work to humanity, a charge taken up by the Calystoans in their mission to break the bonds of servitude and bring about an era of complete freedom for all the people of Aeras.

The Kao'sahetami believe that every person is born possessing a set amount of "Kao" or "Fortune" inside their spirit, which is increased by performing good deeds and reduced when engaging in evil acts. Those with great amounts of Kao are blessed with good luck in their endeavors, while those that are lacking are mired in misfortune. The Kao'sahetami also make use of divination, using the motions and actions of crabs to gauge a person's Kao. The more skilled diviners are said to be able to use the crabs' movement to see the truth of the past, and even predict future events.

Following the collapse of the , the Muka'asubites believed that the Pillars of Virtue had begun to crumble. As the books left behind by the defunct polity began to rot, the devout began carving replacements onto great stone tablets, and burying judges alongside their lawbooks. This, they believe, will equip them with the necessary materials to repair the pillars in the afterlife. As the pillars continue to crumble, however "kebang sejan" are released, spirits of virtue who can possess pious and goodly mortals and dispense great wisdom.

Orgarrdians believe that humanity, which had long been confined to dark caverns and passages, rebelled against their gods in order to reach the surface. The land itself however, had been reduced to ash, scorched by its creators. Instead of freedom, humanity found themselves trapped in cycles of disease, death and grief, alien concepts to their subterranean lives. From that day on, Orgarrdians have sought to find the rock-hewn paradise they were birthed from, constructing underground temples and mining deeper into sacred stone so that the sinful surface does not corrupt their soul.

While veneration of the 'Wizard King' was never popular, Kathanouxa's insistence that he was the rightful god and ruler of aeras was frequently enforced at staff-point and gathered enough of a following over centuries of his rule for it to persist long after his death. Kathanouxiacians believe that Kathanouxa did not die but ascended, and use the mysterious term 'Wizard' for their priesthood in honor of him with hopes their dread master will spare them upon his return.

Ardent monotheists, the Speakers of cnevobarthane are the ancestral religious sect of the Theugombrian people - for although conversion from outsiders is theoretically possible, it has historically been almost unheard of. The Speakers believe that humans live in cyclical eons, where races of men are born, thrive for many long ages, then pass into an unknown and possibly apocalyptic fate. They worship Cnevobarthane, the First Man of the Present World, who was born fully formed from the last remaining stone of the last remaining ruined house of the vanished men of the World Before. He then proceeded to create all mankind, peopling aeras with his progeny. However, many of his children, dissatisfied and jealous, turned their backs upon Him. Only a select few swallowed their pride and, were grateful to their Father, choosing to honor him and his laws. Those who follow him tell of his sacrifice that allowed mankind to avoid the fate of the world before.

When aversaria came for the Theygundarope, the ancient beliefs were lost under the brutal tide of total warfare and genocide. What remains is a legend of the Five Figures, ancient prophets who saved their people from annihilation through subservience to the Purest. For proving their Purity they ascended from this world, joining the Purest in divinity for eternity. Their teachings of Blood Veneration and self-sacrifice are honoured by the many noble families that still trace their lineage to the Five, preserving their legacy through peace and the continual rule of only those blessed with Purity. While the Aversarinas Aagiokratia at large frowned upon this blatant heresy, generations of Governors found coexistence the most profitable, stable course of action.

Kidunawists believe in the Kidunawa, a sea-beneath-the-sea that is home to demons and sinister deities that conspire against all life above water. Calamities that occur at sea are understood as punishment from those that dwell below, leading adherents to constantly seek appeasement by following an array of esoteric superstitions. Some adherents are known to graft parts of sea creatures to their bodies, believing that those who bear part of the deep need not fear the deep.

Aruvasue is built on the worship of the Aspects Of Aruvas, facets of the great Sun-Moon-King, the divine creator of the world. To revere him is to uphold the sanctity of home and hearth, to fulfill the duty of hospitality to a guest of honor, and to never withhold what is offered freely. Adhering to these principles grants passage to the afterlife, where souls tend to the temples of the three realms as divinities themselves.

Oirigan is the religion of the southern mayikrata creole culture of the Samojitians. Descended from slaves who served in the mayikrata's halls and towers at the behest of the magi, the slaves spent years slowly gathering stolen Magical lore and learning the power of Magic for themselves. When Cenware of the Sjalvolki invaded the Mayikrata and shattered the Magi's power, they rose up with the Magi's own gifts to destroy them. The Oirigians worship a flurry of gods inspired by their stolen tomes, and see Magic as a tool needed to secure the freedom of their people.

One of the most ancient cults of aversaria, as old as Aersodiaxynism with several high-profile followers. Believed to be the originator of Aversarian blood worship, the Venerators worship blood as a holy reflection of the power of the gods and strictly believe that the world belongs only to the holiest magi Kings, for they have the purest blood. As such, adherents sought to create the perfect Magi above all else, with some resorting to extreme methods ranging from marriage amongst one's own family to purging the impure, often to the detriment and horror of their own population.

The Kyaali believe that the divine realm, or Kyaal, is a physical place to which one can travel to. They regard it to be where all the world's creatures were born, and thus, all beings bear a splinter of divinity. Some of the Kyaali can perform rituals to harness this splinter, allowing them to wear the skin of any creature to assume their shape, with particular reverence given to those who manage to wear the kyaldzakaxi — fearsome creatures that are hunted in the kyalmuku, or Godhunt.

The Portenchant found themselves conquered and victimized under the lash of the Aversarians, but they did not break in their faith. The Sjalvolki invasion freed them from their servitude, but they do not wish to seek vengeance on their former slavemasters. Instead, they wish to return to the ways of isolationism and quiet faith, as they once practiced when they were free.

The Kidarian Dance, born in the Isles of sarradon, sees all of existence through the lens of rhythm and beats, where their gods are the dancers and the universe is their stage. Natural events, from light breezes to massive thunderstorms, are the gods' steps and moves, and the great cyclical patterns of nature are movements and tempos. Mortals are part of the dance too, though they do not understand the beats they follow in the way that the gods do. Kidarian rituals unsurprisingly center around dancing. Whether to mimic a god's rhythm in thanks and praise, or to use their own rhythms in hope of influencing the world around them, Kidarian priests know a drumbeat for every prayer imaginable. They dedicate themselves in the name of Kidar-awah, the first man to learn the gods' rhythms, and strive to get a glimpse of the divine by dancing in the gods' steps in an endless frenzy. Unfortunately for them, it is more likely to result in bloodied feet and broken ankles than enlightenment.

Uakaklohan faith is a complicated series of thirty gods and thirty one demons (Tekotlaketsuetspallok being a relatively recent addition), that becomes greatly confused due to a belief that the gods can share names, possess multiple forms as different genders or species, and can regularly merge or split. Most of all, however, they worship aervalr, which they claim is the eye of the creator god Tensese ('First One') who gave birth to all others and sent them to aeras while their one eye watched over humanity.

The Islanders of Abdêxorn hold to their ancient heroes in both heart and mind. Famous warriors and magi, legends who forged isles and brought down civilizations without fear or doubt. They unified rivaling city-states with purpose and a warrior's spirit passed down generations. Many of their heroes were ancient enemies of aversaria who eventually bowed to the Purest through bonds of blood. For some, these heroes were traitors, and the chosen of Abdêxorn are destined to bring Aversaria to its knees.

Even though practicing sacrifice has always been a way to ensure that Tensese is never alone despite his children leaving, and the Uakaklohans believing that sacrifices of the willing and prepared will let their souls join their god in the heavens to keep them company for their lonely vigil, the frodbrokna and the great damage done to aervalr was seen as apocalyptic by the Mitikaka Tokollatitzans. The death of most of the Uakaklohans' magi-Kings and their peoples further reinforced this, and Mitikaka Tokollatitzans have begun to accept that the final death of their people is an inevitability, now that even their chief god has been taken from them.

The Gethians entered the known world with the Sjalvolki coalition. Although they tended to be rather insular, they were told by the Sjalvolki of the great evils of the Aversarians and their empire. They hope that by wiping out this despicable and destructive faith, they would be able to redeem themselves in the eyes of the Great Judge Atrākh-Admākh.

The result of centuries of Aversarian laws over Iyrossi worship, the 'Iyrossi Aagiokrata' is the remains of dozens of ancient Iyrossi faiths mixed through forced relocation and the claimed 'reincarnation' of their gods in Aversarian (normally Malcoisiac) figures. While the Iyrossi still hold to some of their old faith through the Timespeakers, even gods are only remembered in Aversarian.

Founded by noblewoman turned preacher Cathrène den va Darrall, Darrallianism is an esoteric blend of beliefs centered around the 'Tripath'; a belief that the only Paths are those of War, Peace and Death. Darrall also claimed that Wayleaders should rule Chevalie as a theocratic class. This and her belief that anyone could be a Wayleader led to many lords converting to establish power through divine right during the War Of The Heretic Princes.

Confined to the isolated Western Isles, the cult of the Biblos'xarians and their hero Bibloseia is relatively unknown to the rest of aversaria. In spite of this, Biblos'xarians are notoriously devoted to their deity, often conducting elaborate ceremonies and libations for the purpose of honoring him. From an early age, children are educated by their elders of Bibloseia's travels and struggles in his journey to obtain the ultimate gift: evading death itself. According to the legend passed down through generations, a young Bibloseia was spoken to one night by the spirit Sautono, promising him that immortality would be his if he killed the frightening death-king Polas-Uro in combat. Sautono warned the young boy that this was a task he would need to devote his life to, for entire armies had groveled at the feet of Polas-Uro before. But Bibloseia was a determined soul. Through many years of training, he became an inspiring warrior, wielding a spear and a unique nine-sided shield he crafted from the finest wood and metal on his home island. Cutting down enemies like a lumberjack topples trees, Bibloseia eventually became strong enough to challenge Polas-Uro, who had kept his strength even in age. And after a duel lasting five hours, Polas-Uro was impaled through the head; the death-king was himself slain. Yet when Bibloseia called upon Sautono to give him immortality, the spirit laughed. Without warning, Bibloseia was instantly turned into stone and cast deep into the earth. The devilish Sautono had given Bibloseia immortality, but not in the way that he wished...

Theryionists worship Theryion, 'the Last Dragon', an ancient Aversarian pretender to the Empire. Grand nephew of Aautokratia Maklea, Theryion was raised in secret with the goal of reclaiming the throne his family believed stolen by Maklea. When Theryion's grandfather died, Theryion at age 20 came out of hiding and assaulted an Aversaria in civil war. Riding a wyvern in place of the (believed) extinct dragons, Theryion began the Widow War, and would quickly become legend with even his enemies naming him 'the Last Dragon' in recognition of his adherence to the old honor and ideals of pre-Maklean Aversaria. The war only ended with his alleged death after nearly 40 years of brutal fighting, Thyrionists claiming that Theryion was a spirit in human form who simply ascended to lay out bloody justice within the heavens. Theryionist belief holds that Theyrion guides the world from the ephemeral First City, and one day shall return, reborn as the Purest to reclaim Their rightful throne.

A millennium of Aversarian rule has seen the rise and failure of innumerable slave revolts on the Isles, often coinciding with whispers of an ancient struggle against the everlasting aautokrata by the enslaved masses. In the chaos of the frodbrokna, a thousand year belief has finally come to light across the isles. The tale always starts with the Fifth, a figure of dissent, long filled with regret. In the shadow of death, the Fifth came to despise the Four, seeing the corruption of tainted blood that nearly saw the end of their people. The Fifth preached to those who would listen of the Truth, a message of betrayal that saw his newfound followers send him to the ancient gods of old. The Five must fall, their descendants slain, the isles freed, their people restored to the lands they once called home, an end to the war that was not lost.

The Kikarsagi were a nomadic tribe wandering the eastern plains until the Aversarians subjugated them. Admiring their new masters' strength, the Kikarsagi largely abandoned their Öltenic religion and adopted the faith that conquered them. Once the frodbrokna hit, however, they came to believe that they could prove their strength and become the Chosen Men themselves through slaughtering the Aversarians.

The Fourteen Heavenly Methods is the name given to the eponymous philosophical tracts attributed to the legendary Empress Yao Ren Cai, also known as the God-Empress or the Dragon Empress. She was a powerful magi, and credited with both unifying the cities of Kathun-Kai and fully subjugating the Miaosi monasteries under the state. During her two-century-long reign, she authored essays on how people of fourteen professions and lifestyles in Kathun-Kai can live virtuously and achieve passage to Heaven. After her death, Yao Ren Cai's soul was said to have merged with the seed of Shenwang to create the first dragon; alternate claims say that her soul possessed the very earth and sky to watch over her people in death. Starting with her son Yao Lingxu, claimants to the throne of Kathun-Kai have claimed to be possessed by the spirit of the first dragon to bolster their legitimacy. Many of these possessed emperors are worshiped alongside Yao Ren Cai, and her Heavenly Methods are rigorously adhered to.

Although responsible for unleashing frodbrokna and destroying aversaria, Aautokratir-Legon Aeschraes#! slowly built a cult of fanatics through the Imperial Civil War and especially in the chaotic decades after that remains insistent that his 'mistakes' were the machinations of dark villains (namely Axiaothea#!). They claim - not without merit - that the throne was his by right and law, and all who call themselves descendants of the Purest must bow to his rule.

The Cult of the Black Hand collapsed and vanished from the public eye after the sudden disappearance of their mysterious leader, the Mindseeker, in 1190. Until their downfall, the Cult had spread like wildfire across Etepezea and portions of Opakhasia, and were most infamous for burning the Nikarynpalati, the millennia-old residence of Aversarian Aautokratirs, to the ground.

'The blood of the weak will sow the land of pure souls.' Such is the mantra of the Black Practitioners. Composed of the vilest Aversarian magi, the Black Practitioners, part of the Black Fraternity, dedicate themselves to one goal only: the casting of the Kusnamenoi, the spell to warp the world and create for themselves a new paradise above it all. Having aroused moral distaste even amongst fellow Aversarians, the Black Practitioners are forced to lay low, their influence mostly concentrated in isolated villages and settlements where they have consolidated their power following the Aversarian collapse. In these villages, no Gods are known but the Magi; these conditions have given way to an acquisitive system of worship focusing on whichever Magus arises as the strongest.

The Praznarvii interpret certain stories about velos drinking the blood of his fallen pray to learn the secrets of life in a literalist context, and take drinking blood to be a sacred and important religious duty. Through this, they believe that they may taste the souls of the dead and learn their secrets, just as their patron god once did.

Across the western Isles, many ships are marked with a strange symbol, three triangles encircled by radiating lines. These vessels carry those who follow the Way of Karoxos, an ancient hero long passed into legend, worshipped in various forms across the seas. Her tales long fallen into myth speak of a grand odyssey across the seven great isles of the early Aversarian age. Be it legends of martial valour in the wars of the Abdexorn, the sack of the three legendary cities of the Symvolix, or an epic duel in the Imperial Isle for love and glory, all tales speak of a calling, a voice hailing Karoxos from distant shores inspiring her journeys. Believers hold that when her fleet finally sailed into the isles she once called home, Karoxos came to understand the voice from afar. A final instruction followed that inspired a thousand years of followers, a request to go forth, sail the seas, and hear the calling for themselves. With a single ship, Karoxos is believed to have sailed into lands untamed, along ancient rivers, and into the lands of freezing winter, a path long followed by the most devout of worshippers.

Propagated by Megistos "the Mad Emperor," this faith is dismissed as a mockery of virtue by those who adhere to traditional Aversarian ways. To adherents, the descendents of the Purest are mentally and physically sound enough to indulge in physical sensations without fear of corruption. But to outsiders, it seems like the ravings of a delusional satyromaniac who says his gods want him to host orgies and bed every beautiful woman in his court.

Founded by the adventurer-king Hasan al-Khour in 727 IS, the Cult of the Monoliths worships three white monoliths engraved with unusually intricate designs and of extremely smooth surface situated in Jubaal-Khour. Claiming that the monoliths gave commandments to their most faithful followers when smeared in blood, ritual bloodletting swiftly evolved into the sacrifice of criminals to the monoliths, which purportedly increases the intensity of their commandments, as they are almost inaudible when given only offerings of blood. The monoliths are believed to represent a chthonic three-headed god of life and death, who was the first being to ascend the 1001 Steps of where he became King of all Gods.

Named by its followers after its eponymous founder and leader Gorassos#!, the Aversarian faith has been crudely reshaped into a cult of war and death. Purity is not earned by the blood in one's veins, but how much heathen blood one spills. And Gorassos intended for #EMP much#! blood to be spilled in his name.

The religion of the northern slaves of the former mayikrata, Dejorig beliefs primarily descend from those slaves who were put to work excavating and exploring the dark tunnels running throughout the mayikrata, a task which would kill nearly every other man and woman sent to it. Its followers reject magic entirely, seeing it as a foul evil and whispering harshly about its effect on what lies below the Citadel. To the Dejorigs, magic is the source of the world's evil, and may be the path to its end.

Tšuekanists worship Tšuek'a, the One-Clad-In-Black, who taught man the arts of enchantment and rune-making before falling into a deep slumber. They believe that his sleeping form can be seen to this very day, and have an uncanny sense of its location at all times. Tšuekanist runes also state that Tšuek'a instructed the "First Shapers", though the knowledge of what they had shaped is now long lost to time.

The Monokerixi, while rejecting the divinity of aautokratirs and holding the Saints to be the only incarnation of the Purest, have still been heavily influenced by traditional Aversarian cultural beliefs. They hold that it was no accident that the Saints chose to manifest in Aversaria, but rather due to the Aversarians' advanced culture increasing their ability to follow the Saints' messages and achieve purity. As such, it is the duty of pious Agionists to spread both Agionist teachings and Aversarian knowledge to the lesser peoples of the world, so that all may benefit from the teachings of the Saints.

'Followers' of the Mountain-Sun Temple, as they refer to themselves as, worship the mountain now referred to as the . Follower doctrine claims that there are twenty major gods and eighty spirits and demons, and their esoteric methods are far too complicated and intricate for anyone to understand unless they have dedicated themselves to studying them. Followers believe that nobody but one who understands them may rule, and historically the Mountain-Sun Temple was entirely ruled by a theocratic class of monastic orders, the chief one located at the itself. When the Mountain-Sun Temple, both the religion and the realm, were conquered by the Aversarians, many of their beliefs and structures were incorporated into their religion. Most notably, the Mountain-Sun Temple still technically exists as the 'Scarlet Chosen', an Aversarian order of warrior-monks protecting the temples.

The 'Coast Path' is an exceptionally loose gathering of several dozen separate faiths that are native to the Konijkmener coasts as well as southeastern chevalie. The fusing of native beliefs in oceanic and river based pantheons with the Lifepath, it enjoyed several hundred years of relative isolation before being stamped out brutally by Woder Broudan of the Konijkmener in 772 IS as part of his attempts to centralize the realm. With much of the faith destroyed and little interest or need for it in Chevalie, the Chevaliens soon after began their own process of rooting out the native faiths, though pockets have doggedly survived throughout the centuries.

For the followers of the Sacred Stars, the heroes of the Isles exist for a time far greater than their life on aeras. Each star is an ascended legend of old, each constellation a sign for their followers.
