Worship is what draws them back to the lands they once called home, the brighest stars are those still treasured by their followers on Aeras. The most celebrated and beloved heroes live in the ancient halls of aervalr, watching, endowing their most loyal adherants with blessings in life and a chance of eternal existence.

The Slaves of the God of Pain believe that the world was created by the malevolent god Ashred min Bashitan al-Atilaam. When he created the world, he tore off his own flesh to make the plants, while he ground his bones to dust to fill the hollow sphere he used to create the earth, which became the soil and metals of the earth. He then created animals by burning himself, and he created the race of men by tearing out his own heart. Finally, he used his spine to erect and the Spires of Woe. Having invested so much of himself into the world, he gave the first men a simple commandment: go forth and slaughter. As more people are slain and suffer, the Pain God will regenerate his body. The legends of the Slaves have it that Ashred was worshiped long ago by an empire of mighty warriors, but their empire crumbled and, bereft of regular sacrifices, Ashred punished man severely. To prevent further punishments, the Slaves must suffer so that others do not.

Worship of Abathon, ancient prophet, god-king and creator of the Isles has existed for millennia. Abathonics are mysterious but are known to believe their godly magi ascended after a mysterious journey to each Wayline Isle, somehow surviving their immense power and creating the ritual of 'Soulbirth' to obtain immortality. Only fragments remain, but legends say the ritual involves destroying a child's soul and granting the empty vessel to Abathon. While believers are oft ridiculed for their beliefs, the Aversarians have lost many expeditions searching for the truth within Abathon's legends.

'The one who lies in wait, the lingerer, the serene, the forebearer, the resolute, the one who awaits the coming of Ch'ngluiyarog, the sundering and rebirth of this wretched world. Praise be to Tagognth, God of the Bitter Wave!' The cultists of the Bitter Wave are without exception regarded as an eerie people possessing utterly outlandish beliefs by their fellows. Given to the worship of the mysterious god Tagognth, whose true name is privy to none save only the most highly ranking of their priestly strata, the Cultists of the Bitter Wave practice a set of exceptionally unsettling rituals they believe are required to commune with the strange god of the sea. The blood of man is a common component, though other strange materials such as unusual skulls that resemble a sister group of humanity are also used from time to time. How or where they obtain such skulls and other strange curiosities is unknown. Priests and laymen both don helmets and armor of a make similar to no other culture on aeras, confounding scholars who question the purpose of such attire. Adding to the mystifying nature of this cult are the frequent but oft dismissed reports by fishermen of the Isles that an immense being lurks beneath the sea, countless colorless eyes all pointed towards the surface. Watching... and waiting.

The followers of this isolationist cult are feared across the Isles for their fanaticism, their faith only known to a few traders and dusty shelves in Aversarian academies. Followers disdain the peoples of the isles, seeing them as complicit in the great betrayal that has haunted them ever since. They speak of a guardian eagle, protectors of a meek people blessed to live under its wings. They were safe, until a jealous magi cut the throat of their guardian and left them to the terrors of the night. In time they grew strong, and when the time was right they set sail to find a home where they could mourn their protector in peace. Since ancient times their isles have found peace, most never encountering an eagle banner in their lifetime. Once in a century however tales spread far and wide of a small fleet sailing the waves, slaying any Magi unfortunate enough to cross their path.

The rare unity forged by Guillate forever cemented his legacy in the greater Isles, a most beloved hero of all islander cults. For his most loyal followers however, Guillate is something far greater. In death, believers hold that Guillate passed beyond the lands of heroes, ascending to rule over the pantheon's of his newly forged empire for eternity. Contempt has long festered amongst the Followers for the peoples of the isles, in their eyes betrayers of Guillate's legacy in their destruction of his empire and denial of his high status. The frodbrokna in their eyes is Guillate's long awaited divine punishment, righeous retribution for the islanders's ignorance and lack of humility. Radicalisation and militarisation has followed, as their path has been made clear through a most divine revelation of a most holy war. Followers seek an end to the worship of lesser heroes, to cast the idolatry of false heroes into the sea and offer the isles to Guillate as a gesture of submission to the supreme hero.

The 'Mothers and Fathers' refers to the collective rituals and practices of the Clanlander tribes that have survived through the ages as memory and history of their past. These stories are collected, generally in song form, and are meant to be carried by each generation onto the next to protect both their legacy and the Cycle Of The Wood.

Thoronis, either a Dragon or a magi who once ruled the northern lands of aeras is the subject of much reverence amongst the peoples of the Continent. A faith sprung forth from the shores of the bay now named after Pathos, some Aversarian scholars assert that it may in fact be the furthest northern branch of Aversarian religions, though it is still distinct from the Aersodiaxianists of Aelarvisia and the Aagiokratans of the latter day empire. The self-proclaimed Listeners of Thoronis are of the belief that the eponymous god Thoronis gave rise to the oceans in an expression of their rage against the denizens of the seafloor who refused to offer worship to them. To avert such tragedies from occurring again, adherents regularly make offerings in the form of immense ships bearing sacrifices to the sea to appease the god, who will in return give gifts such as calm seas and bountiful harvests. It is rumored that these sacrificial vessels sometimes bear humans in times of famine and stormy weather, when their god is thought to be greatly angered, although the existence of such practices is vehemently denied by Thoronites.

Humanity is hopelessly corrupt, and attempts to save mankind from impurity are futile without the Saints. Therefore the faithful seek isolation from the corruption of the outside world to preserve the Saintly teachings until the 'Second Manifestation', when the Saints shall return to spread their divine teachings as they did in times long past.

A Sarradonian legend says ten-thousand years ago, the warlords of Sarradon fell to decadence and wickedness, and the Pillars holding the Dead God in Iblis had begun to crumble. When all seemed lost, brave and righteous men emerged from the Shadows to cull these false warlords, and disappeared once again into the shadows. Whether this legend is true or not, the Haradari'un have had several foundings and disbandings over the millenia, to the point that nobody truly knows how old the order of assassins is. The most recent founding occurred not long after frodbrokna. This new founding has concluded that after Aeschraes broke the world, no magi can be allowed to live. They are concerned primarily with the hunting down and assassination of Magi, especially those with godlike power.

The cultists of the Distant Shore are a rare sight in aeras, seldom seen beyond the scattered Isles of its devoted adherents. They preach of a land of heroes far beneath the waves, a distant shore where paradise awaits. Only the enigmatic preacher known as the 'Listener' can commune with the ancient heroes who live beyond the shore, an ability passed down from preacher to preacher through elaborate ritual. When the Listener finally hears their call, the most virtuous gather in celebration for their final journey. With the Listener's blessing of protection and the guidance of the heroes before, a small fleet sets sail for the Distant Shore, passing through lands unknown to even the most experienced Aversarian sailor in a grand adventure to the land beyond the waves.

At the edge of the island remnants of aversaria, the ancient cult of the Cleansed has reemerged in the wake of frodbrokna, preaching a new message of restoration in the midst of general anarchy. Several Isles have fallen under their sway through bloody uprisings and brutal conquests, often followed by mass purges of those deemed unwilling or unable to understand the calamity adherants hold was spawned of a milenium of Aversarian degeneracy. Believers speak of an ancient ritual known only as the Blood Trial, a trial that allows a magi to be purified, false beliefs eradicated, their blood cleansed, and a path to the Purest made clear. This path is said to be a blessing from the highest figure of the cult, the so-called 'Voice of the Purest'. The few exiles that have left the cult since the Frodbrokna speak of a depraved ritual of self-flagellation, complete Magi supremacy, and the widespread enslavement of non-magic commoners.

Adherents of this faith consider the Orn'daehz, or 'Goldkin', as their gods and masters, who themselves were just as diverse as humanity despite their great power and fell after breaking into two warring tribes. They believe that the Goldkin were then destroyed by the 'Skywoman', who banished them to a land without love or passion. Thus, the Orn'daehz partake in the pleasures of the flesh, conduct ritualistic raids and imbibe strange euphoria-inducing elixirs in order to channel their sensations as energies to the 'other land' — believing that given enough energy, the Goldkin will be able to escape their exile. The Druhan'daehzag also believe in a holy state of being called the 'Lifematch', where two men or two women claim another to be the missing half of their soul and (in an attempt to prevent the ancient schism that destroyed the Goldkin) declare that their spirits are forever one, refusing to partake in any form of pleasure without the other present.

The practitioners of Ethowos of are the descendants of the dispossessed, slaves escaped from Aversarian control. They claimed to have been led by Ethowo, a giant Axolotl which broke free from the containment of Averserian magi, and in so doing, broke open several slave enclosures. Ethowo is worshipped as a god by the Ethowai, who bring it vast quantities of food from the surface.

A faith found in the long-conquered but ever restive territory of Saielai, Bayn Huac Driem is fundamentally a form of matriarchal ancestor worship. Adherents are expected to keep shrines honoring the lineage of their mothers and grandmothers, with wealthier patrons being expected to produce more elaborate offerings. In particular, lifelike portraiture and sculpture are held in high esteem, and has resulted in the Saielai producing some of the east's most renowned portrait artists and sculptors. Their best work, of course, has always gone towards honoring their mothers' shrines.

Originating from the Syaidan valleys, the northeastern borderland of Kathun-Kai, the Thuantyan Sraidyan — or Eternal Commune — teaches that the universe was built by the efforts of a half dozen Eternal Stewards, each of whom were once mortal men and women who ascended from Aeras to leave their mark upon the heavens. By emulating a Steward in life, a suitably virtuous and attentive worshipper may achieve immortality by literally becoming that Steward in death. As the faith's missionaries have followed along the trade routes southwest into Kathun-Kai, the faith has taken root in that great empire, thriving in spite of periods of persecution at the hands of the government.

To the Dahalai, power, much like gold or water, is a concrete resource that can be gained or spent. Its faithful believe that the best way to obtain power is through the cleansing of one's body through meditation and abstinence. As one then accumulates power, they manifest desirable qualities — and those that possess great beauty and fertility are honored as paragons.

To the followers of the , the Twin Gods’ prohibition on slavery includes the act of forcing outsiders to embrace their way of life. Thus, while they are respected by all Nattanan, adherents mostly keep to their own within the deepest reaches of the jungle. Gotharnalmics also shun war and violence as a corrupting force that caused Korlacu to fall and believe that the only time it is permitted by the Twin Gods is when it is done to protect one’s own.

A remote sect centered in the northwestern mountains of Dahangon, the Saldakai maintain that power is not only concrete but also tangible in the form of magic. Practitioners of this sect seek to accumulate magical energies within their bodies, and Saldakai rituals are heavily pervaded by the arcane.

An oft-persecuted magi cult originating from the border garrison towns of Kathun-Kai, Mogudorism grew out of the practices of a sect of war-magi who perfected the art of binding a variety of temperamental and vile spirits to use as weapons. While the faith has been permanently proscribed by the Kathuni state due to their involvement in a coup attempt in the eighth century IS, every attempt to burn their teachings has proven ultimately futile; copies of the Chaining Manual and its supplemental volumes have a way of reappearing under mysterious circumstances.

The , preaches that the best way to honor the Twin Gods is through being in harmony with nature and conducting holy hunts in their name. Adherents view outsiders as melancholic and misguided souls and believe that it is the duty of the faithful to convince them to walk the middle path. Despite this openness however, the Nathanalmics continue to be wary of ideas outside the norm, out of fear that these may ignite anew the tragedies that passed before.

While the orthodox teachings of the Dahalan faith preach that power is obtained through purity, the Narinai believe that power is already present in one's body, and must be freed from the passions that contaminate it. To this end, adherents constantly strive to exhaust these passions — whether through revelry, deviancy or even murder. This has led the Narinai to be persecuted in Dahangon, though it has taken root in the land and is oft practiced by ambitious lords seeking dominion.

The Sokaloma is the traditional faith of the Takopara, a belief system which holds that the stars are the fundamental element to human existence. To those that venerate the Sokaloma or 'sky dance' each star represents a person's past and future being, believing that when one dies they leave behind their previous soul among the sky, in order to be reincarnated from the very same matter. Sokalomans hold that this fabric of consciousness manifests itself into figures which are more akin to traditional deities, represented by the various constellations of the night sky. One's fate and guardian spirits are determined at birth through complex astrological rituals only understood through years of meticulous study.

A divergent sect of the Stranûni faith practiced by the Kirinarth traders that would later become the Dathanites, the Labaqirites believe that the source of power within the Holy Mountain of Dahangon is none other than the Dead God itself, imprisoned within its inaccessible depths. Inheriting the musical beliefs of the Stranûnites, practitioners of the sect have a variety of chants and songs of warding, and during solstices and eclipses, hold festivals with a cacophony of noises to drive away evil.

Sagadavians typically practice the native faith of Zaldrigas, or serpent worship. The belief that in the distant past the great winged serpent Balabalos consumed the old world and its people, only for the new world to burst free nine days later, now cleansed by Balabalos's celestial grace. It is said that this traumatic event was but just one of many, and the cycle of rebirth shall continue for all eternity. In prayer Zaldrigan worshippers give thanks to Balabalos, but also recognize a whole host of spirits, gods, and lesser divinities birthed from the great serpent.

Karan 'the Dessicated God' is the ancient corpse of an unknown being unearthed by the mountain tribes. While undoubtedly dead, it still emits magical energies powerful enough to kill all who draws near. Worshipping it as divine, the Karannai dream of ascending to divinity and preserving the dying world, something they can allegedly attain by consuming a piece of the dead god.

To the Oulaupi, the eight highest peaks of the Northern Mountains represent the eight gods and their heavens, with the tallest peak 'Nauvreg' being the last and the greatest. Scaling the eight peaks is considered to be the supreme act of devotion, and only those who have scaled up to the fifth heaven are considered fit to be chieftains. Their veneration for higher altitudes lead the Oulaupi to practice a peculiar custom of hanging the coffins of their deceased upon the mountainside, and they fiercely forbid all outsiders to approach these elevated graveyards.

After a great disaster tore off a large portion of Nauvreg's peak, some of the mountain tribes ceased to consider it as the greatest heaven, venerating instead what was once the second highest peak, Cosnfregl. Adherents of the faith have a penchant for sacrificing captives in altars constructed around the mountain, believing that the rituals will replenish the red ore they often mine in Cosnfregl's depths.

The Solungvendi place great importance in the purity of the self, which they seek to achieve through deep meditation. Adherents often seek isolated peaks and chasms, preferably those with minimal footholds, as they believe that breaking free from one's physical attachment to aeras is necessary to achieve true tranquility. Their trances often last for days on end, with naught but the cold winds keeping them company.

A melding of Wardenite religious practices with some traditions endemic to the Tarsanides mountains, Kothori'a brings a number of ancient gods from the old Barzawa pantheons into the faith as either benevolent spiritual guardians or saints, with the first group often incarnating as important judges, and the latter often ascending upon death to join to a near-divine status. This is naturally quite scandalous to the broader Wardenite faith, which is noted for a distinct anti-theist stance, but the Kothorites have always skirted suppression largely due to the remoteness of their mountainous homes, and the relative obscurity of their rural faith.

Across the villages of the far west Isles, when the stars and moon align, an almost eerie silence befalls the isles of those who believe themselves Favoured. Homes fall silent, ships left to the waves, as communities gather in various stone circles vast and ancient. Each stone is uniquely marked, be it a painting, a symbol, even old words once spoken in wisdom. In this circle of legends followers remember those of the past, each heroes and legends in their time. Tales of strength, wisdom and glory are spoken, prayers whispered and stories passed onto future descendants. Each hero is remembered, a ritual to receive their blessing for the years to come. The most ancient and celebrated heroes are ancient ancestors once endowed with Favour by their God, freed from long forgotten masters and blessed with new lands to settle. Believers hold that their Favour was a divine gift not so easily lost to time. As long as they are not forgotten, their Favour may be found once more, the hope for a new hero for the ages to venerate and remember.

The Wyrd Path was born from a mix of early proselytizing efforts by marchers in northern Kalathipsomi. It would prove only marginally successful, teaching the necessity of following your path through life, but failing to replace their ancient rites and customs.

Worshipping a vast pantheon of spirits and fire-gods, the Ēbohephai consider the earth sacred due to their unique religious heritage. Burying people in kurgans, they give worship to Arlikhaan and his five thousand demon-spirits and one hundred fire gods.
